sound_type: rolling mek-mek-mek + chatter -- the open-country corvid, rolling nasal staccato, social chatter, percussive quality
ecosystem: Open country -- sagebrush steppe, agricultural fields, riparian corridors, ranch lands. Primarily east of the Cascades.
season: Year-round resident. Does not migrate. Breeds in spring. Social -- typically found in family groups or loose flocks.

coordinates.description: Reference for open country habitat in eastern Washington. Black-billed Magpies found in sagebrush steppe, agricultural fields, and riparian corridors primarily east of the Cascades.

taxonomy_note.description: SEVENTH CONSECUTIVE CORVIDAE (100-106) -- the Corvidae Septet. Pica is a different genus from all previous corvids in the engine (Corvus, Cyanocitta, Perisoreus, Nucifraga). NEW GENUS. Formerly considered conspecific with the European Magpie (Pica pica); split to Pica hudsonia by the AOU in 2000.
taxonomy_note.family_count_in_engine: Corvidae count: 8 (Steller's Jay 53, Raven 100, Raven pair 101, American Crow 102, Steller's Jay raucous 103, Gray Jay 104, Clark's Nutcracker 105, Black-billed Magpie 106)
taxonomy_note.name_etymology: Pica from Latin pica ('magpie'). hudsonia = 'of the Hudson Bay region,' referencing the type specimen's origin. 'Magpie' from 'Mag' (diminutive of Margaret, a chatterer) + 'pie' (from Latin pica). The name reflects the bird's vocal nature.
taxonomy_note.genus_note: Pica is new to the engine at degree 106. Two species in North America: Black-billed Magpie (P. hudsonia, widespread western) and Yellow-billed Magpie (P. nuttalli, California endemic). The Black-billed Magpie was split from the European Magpie (P. pica) in 2000.
taxonomy_note.order_note: Passeriformes. Seventh consecutive corvid passerine at degrees 100-106.
taxonomy_note.family_position: Corvidae continues its unprecedented run. Seven consecutive degrees (100-106) breaks the Mustelidae sextet (63-68) and the Sciuridae sextet (75-80) ties. Now the second-longest family streak in the engine, behind only Sciuridae's octet (75-82).

song_metrics.fundamental_frequency_range_hz: 1,500-5,000 Hz. Rolling, nasal, staccato mek calls. Broadband energy with harsh spectral characteristics.
song_metrics.peak_energy_band_hz: 2,000-4,000 Hz. The rolling quality comes from rapid repetition of nasal staccato notes.
song_metrics.song_frequency_hz: Primary mek-mek-mek: 2,000-4,500 Hz, nasal, staccato, rapid. Chatter: broadband, rapid-fire, overlapping. Alarm: harsher, more emphatic mek calls.
song_metrics.song_duration_s: Individual mek: 0.1-0.3 seconds. Series: 1-5 seconds of rolling mek-mek-mek. Chatter sessions: can persist for minutes when multiple birds are involved.
song_metrics.note_structure: ROLLING NASAL MEK: Rapid series of short, harsh, nasal notes -- mek mek mek mek. Staccato delivery. Rolling pace. Conversational and social rather than a single long call. The rolling quality is the diagnostic acoustic.
song_metrics.note_types: Mek-mek-mek (primary, social/contact), chatter (social, agitated), alarm (harsher mek variants), quiet calls (close-range pair communication). Highly social -- multiple birds vocalizing simultaneously is common.
song_metrics.phrase_structure: Rolling series. The mek-mek-mek is delivered in rapid bursts with short pauses. No fixed phrase structure. Social and conversational. Multiple birds creating overlapping vocal textures.
song_metrics.timbre: Nasal. Rolling. Percussive. Staccato. The most rapid-fire timbre in the Corvidae septet. Distinct from the raven's resonance, the crow's sharpness, and the nutcracker's grating. The magpie is percussive and chattering.
song_metrics.amplitude: E=5 -- high energy. Loud, frequent, persistent. The rolling mek-mek-mek carries across open country. Social flocks create walls of overlapping chatter. Third E=5 corvid in the septet.
song_metrics.tempo: Fast. The mek-mek-mek is delivered at a rapid rolling pace. The chatter is rapid-fire. The magpie is the fastest vocalizer in the corvid septet.
song_metrics.context: SOCIAL AND CONVERSATIONAL. Contact calls between family members, social chatter in flocks, alarm calls, territorial vocalizations. The magpie is among the most vocal corvids -- calling frequently throughout the day. Social cohesion through constant vocal contact.
song_metrics.regional_variation: Moderate. Some geographic variation in call structure. Individual variation present. The rolling nasal quality is consistent across range.
song_metrics.key_property: ROLLING MEK-MEK-MEK + CHATTER -- the open-country corvid. E=5. Rolling, nasal, staccato, percussive. Bold black-and-white plumage. Tail longer than body. Dome-shaped nests unique among corvids. Open country east of Cascades. SEVENTH CONSECUTIVE CORVIDAE (100-106). The Corvidae Septet. Forty-sixth consecutive original CSV.

plumage.overall: Large, long-tailed corvid. Bold black-and-white plumage. Iridescent black (green-bronze-purple) head, breast, back, and wings. White belly and scapulars. White wing patches. Extraordinarily long iridescent tail -- longer than the body.
plumage.diagnostic_mark: For degree 106: (1) EXTRAORDINARILY LONG TAIL -- longer than the body, iridescent green-bronze. Unmistakable silhouette. (2) BOLD BLACK-AND-WHITE -- stark contrast pattern unlike any other corvid. (3) IRIDESCENT -- green, bronze, purple sheen on black plumage in good light. (4) OPEN COUNTRY -- if you see a black-and-white bird with a streaming long tail in open country east of the Cascades, it is a Black-billed Magpie.
plumage.seasonal_variation: Minimal. Year-round plumage. Juveniles have shorter tails and duller plumage, acquiring full adult plumage by first winter.
plumage.flight_pattern: Undulating, somewhat labored flight with the long tail streaming behind. White wing patches flash in flight. The long tail makes the bird immediately identifiable in flight. Often seen flying low across open country.

physical_description.overall: Large corvid. Body length 45-60 cm (including tail). Wingspan 56-61 cm. Weight 145-210 g. Bold black-and-white plumage. Iridescent black with green-bronze-purple sheen. Long tail (25-30 cm) longer than body.
physical_description.diagnostic_mark: For degree 106: black-and-white bird with an extraordinarily long iridescent tail in open country. The tail is the key -- no other PNW bird has this silhouette. Dome-shaped stick nests in trees are also diagnostic.
physical_description.field_id: Large black-and-white bird with a long streaming tail in open country east of the Cascades. Rolling mek-mek-mek call. If you see a bold black-and-white bird with a tail longer than its body in sagebrush steppe or agricultural fields -- it is a Black-billed Magpie.

nodes.title: Rolling Mek-Mek-Mek + Chatter -- The Open-Country Corvid
nodes.description: Rolling, nasal, staccato mek-mek-mek. E=5. Loud, frequent, persistent. Social chatter creates walls of overlapping corvid conversation. The most percussive and rapid-fire voice in the Corvidae septet.

nodes.title: Seventh Consecutive Corvidae (100-106) -- The Corvidae Septet
nodes.description: Raven (100, deep cronk), Raven pair (101, duet), American Crow (102, sharp caw), Steller's Jay (103, raucous alarm), Gray Jay (104, soft whistles), Clark's Nutcracker (105, harsh grating), Black-billed Magpie (106, rolling mek-mek-mek). Seven degrees, one family. Breaks Mustelidae/Sciuridae sextet ties. Second-longest streak (after Sciuridae octet 75-82).

nodes.title: Dome-Shaped Nests -- Unique Among Corvids
nodes.description: Massive enclosed structures of sticks and mud with a roof. 60-120 cm diameter. 40-50 days to build. Reused by other species (owls, hawks, ducks). Community infrastructure. Unique among corvids worldwide.

nodes.title: Bold Black-and-White -- Long Tail Longer Than Body
nodes.description: Iridescent black (green-bronze-purple) and white plumage. Tail longer than body -- the most visually striking corvid in the engine. Unmistakable silhouette in flight. The long tail is diagnostic.

nodes.title: Open Country -- East of the Cascades
nodes.description: Sagebrush steppe, agricultural fields, riparian corridors. Primarily east of the Cascades. Shared habitat with Red Fox (71) and Townsend's Ground Squirrel (78). The broadest-scale landscape in the corvid septet.

cross_references.relationship: CORVIDAE SEPTET (100-106). Nutcracker in alpine pine (E=5 harsh grating); magpie in open country (E=5 rolling mek-mek-mek). Both E=5 but entirely different habitats and acoustic qualities.

CORVIDAE SEPTET (100-106). Intelligence. Both highly intelligent corvids. The raven is widespread; the magpie is an open-country specialist. The magpie builds dome nests; the raven builds open platforms. The magpie may pass the mirror self-recognition test.
CORVIDAE SEPTET (100-106). The crow is urban/generalist; the magpie is open-country/specialist. Both bold and social. Maximum habitat overlap in suburban/agricultural transition zones.
OPEN COUNTRY SHARED HABITAT. Both found in dry open landscapes east of the Cascades. The fox hunts; the magpie scavenges and forages. Shared open-country community.
OPEN COUNTRY SHARED HABITAT. Ground squirrel is prey in the open-country food web; magpie is omnivore/scavenger. Both signature species of dry steppe east of the Cascades.
